activity_title: Discovery sweep - no existing returns dashboard; confirmed build not extend
activity_summary: Swept the repo, git history, the wider filesystem and the database for any existing returns/refund dashboard before writing anything. Repo was empty (zero commits, unborn main). Found two returns views in staging_ai (amazon_fbm_returns_opportunity_engine_v1 and v_returns_ph_board_feed_proposal_v1) but both are Amazon FBM only, so neither answers the Amazon+eBay requirement. Found the sibling POSTAGE-RECONCILIATION-SYSTEM carrying the three TABLE_*.md table skills the requirement claimed were already in this project but were not. Also found amazon_returns and ebay_returns carry NO indexes at all.
reusable_pattern: Discovery before build - search repo, git, filesystem AND database views for an existing asset before creating a competing one, to avoid duplicate truth
gap_or_risk: Requirement claimed reference files were already in the project; they were not. Neither existing staging_ai view covers eBay.
next_action: Build fresh; reuse the sibling project's table skills and folder conventions

activity_title: DR-001 - the requirement's eBay SKU join double-counts refunds by +12.7%
activity_summary: The requirement's Step 4 joins ebay_returns to order_transaction on order_id alone. order_transaction is a LINE-ITEM table, so a returned order containing several basket items fans out and the same refund is counted once per SKU. Measured on live data: 489 return rows become 581 after the join; 42 orders fan out; worst case one order across 10 line items. This inflates the eBay SKU refund total from a true 11384.44 to 12825.91 (+12.7%) AND blames innocent SKUs that merely shared a basket with a faulty item - which corrupts the exact decision the report exists to drive.
evidence_refs: 489 rows -> 581 after join; 42 fan-out orders; max 10 line items; naive total 12825.91 vs true 11384.44 = +12.7%; item_id join resolves 440/489 (90%) to one SKU, 46 variation listings, 3 unmatched (74.79)
reusable_pattern: Any table named *_transaction or *_item is probably LINE-ITEM grain. Joining a return/refund/event table to it on order_id alone is a fan-out. Always check the grain of the right-hand table before joining - this will recur on Shopify, Wayfair, B&Q.
validation_rule: Resolve eBay SKU by joining order_transaction ON order_id AND item_id, never order_id alone. Where one item_id maps to several SKUs (variation listing) split refund and units evenly. Where no order line matches, emit an explicit UNATTRIBUTED row - never silently drop the refund.
gap_or_risk: DWC specified the original SQL. The fix overrides him and is not yet ratified.
next_action: DWC to ratify the corrected join

activity_title: The requirement's Amazon SQL sums four currencies into one number labelled GBP
activity_summary: The requirement's Steps 1 and 2 run SUM(refunded_amount) with no currency grouping. Amazon in this window carries GBP, USD, EUR, CAD and 677 rows with a NULL currency. The requirement's SQL therefore adds pounds to dollars to euros and prints the result under a pound-sign header, producing a meaningless headline of 36899.74. This is the exact error the requirement's own scope table forbids - it simply never noticed Amazon is itself multi-currency. Every query now partitions by currency and the dashboard scopes to one currency at a time.
evidence_refs: Amazon per-currency: GBP 21412.33 (1205 rows), UNSPECIFIED 6082.71 (677), USD 4638.24 (121), EUR 4362.85 (182), CAD 403.61 (14); requirement's ungrouped SQL yields 36899.74 labelled GBP
reusable_pattern: Multi-currency is not only a cross-platform problem - a single marketplace can be multi-currency internally. Any SUM over a money column without a currency GROUP BY is a bug until proven otherwise.
validation_rule: IF aggregating any refund figure THEN GROUP BY currency, always. Never sum across currencies and never sum Amazon with eBay.

activity_title: Amazon reason codes carry a CR- prefix inconsistently - grouping raw splits one reason in two
activity_summary: Live Amazon reason codes mostly carry a CR- prefix, but the same logical reason appears both with and without it (CR-NOT_COMPATIBLE and NOT_COMPATIBLE are the same reason from the same feed). Grouping on the raw column splits one reason across two rows and both look smaller than they are. The prefix is now stripped mechanically with regexp_replace(reason,'^CR-',''). No reason was renamed or merged beyond that. Separately, the reason lists printed in the requirement were wrong for BOTH platforms - eBay's real codes (WRONG_SIZE, ORDERED_ACCIDENTALLY, NO_LONGER_NEED_ITEM) barely overlap with the documented ones.
evidence_refs: NOT_COMPATIBLE GBP: 347 returns / 5665.27 after normalisation; requirement's ungrouped reason query returns 34 rows vs 17 after stripping; eBay actual codes differ from the requirement's list
reusable_pattern: Enum-ish text columns drift. The same value arrives both prefixed and unprefixed from the same feed. Normalise before grouping or the same category silently splits into two rows.
validation_rule: Strip the CR- prefix from amazon_returns.reason before any GROUP BY.
gap_or_risk: AMZ-PG-BAD-DESC left as its own reason - see D01-A08

activity_title: Built the self-contained HTML report from real pulled data
activity_summary: Executed all four queries against live PostgreSQL and embedded the real result rows in a single self-contained HTML file - CSS, JS and data inline, zero external references. Four sections in the required order (Amazon reasons, Amazon SKUs, eBay reasons, eBay SKUs), header with title/date/snapshot disclaimer, top-3 rows shaded, all tables sorted by refund descending. The three SQL deviations were disclosed in a panel on the page itself so the team lead sees them without being told. Stretch goal delivered: every top SKU shows its most common return reason.
evidence_refs: GBP headline: Amazon 1205 returns / 21412.33 (top reason NOT_COMPATIBLE 5665.27); eBay 340 returns / 7742.62 (top reason WRONG_SIZE 1885.89); rendered and screenshotted in a browser
reusable_pattern: Disclose deviations from a requirement ON the artefact itself, not in a side email - the reviewer sees them without being told and cannot miss them.

activity_title: Zero-trust revalidation - re-ran every query and diffed 896 cells against the HTML
activity_summary: Refused to trust the prior build. Re-ran all four queries from scratch and diffed the results cell-by-cell in code against what the HTML actually embedded - 896 cells across 206 rows. Every value matched. Re-verified every claim the report makes about itself (2199/489/5064 row counts, 42 fan-out orders, 12825.91 vs 11384.44, +12.7%, 440/46/3 item_id resolution). All exact. The only discrepancy found was a tie-ordering artefact, not a data error - see D01-A11.
evidence_refs: 896 cells compared, 887 exact, 9 differences all confined to tie-ordering of three zero-refund rows; all self-claims re-verified exact
reusable_pattern: Zero-trust revalidation - re-derive from source and diff programmatically, never eyeball. A report that cannot be re-derived is not validated.
validation_rule: Before delivery, re-run every query and diff the result set against the artefact cell by cell in code.
gap_or_risk: Revealed the undisclosed Amazon refund coverage gap - see D01-A07
next_action: Surface the coverage gap to DWC

activity_title: GAP - Amazon refund coverage is 42.7% incomplete and the dashboard does not say so
activity_summary: 940 of 2199 Amazon returns carry refunded_amount = NULL. In GBP that is 313 of 1205 (26%). They contribute zero to the report not because they were free but because the field is empty. Only 9 of the 940 are recoverable from amz_order_expenses (555.30), so this is upstream data incompleteness, not a query bug. The KPI card shows 'Total Returns 1,205' beside 'Total Refund 21,412.33', which reads as though all 1205 are costed - 892 are. It also depresses the SKU ranking: a SKU whose returns all lack a refund value ranks at 0.00 despite being genuinely returned. Company's own TABLE_amazon_returns.md warns refunded_amount is a denormalised summary. Fix is a one-line caption on the KPI card; NOT YET APPLIED.
evidence_refs: 940/2199 NULL (42.7%); GBP 313/1205 (26%); UNSPECIFIED 543/677 (80.2%); CAD 10/14 (71.4%); only 9 rows recoverable from amz_order_expenses = 555.30; eBay by contrast has ZERO nulls
reusable_pattern: A NULL in a money column is not a zero. A dashboard that sums NULLs into a total silently under-reports and ranks innocent SKUs at 0.00. ALWAYS report coverage alongside a monetary total.
validation_rule: When publishing any monetary total, publish the coverage (n rows with a value / n rows total) beside it.
gap_or_risk: HIGH - the report understates Amazon cost and invites a false read. Undisclosed on the page.
next_action: Add a coverage caption to the Amazon KPI card; raise with DWC

activity_title: GAP - return rate is NOT computable for Amazon; SKU key does not match order_transaction
activity_summary: Return rate (returns divided by units sold) is the metric the business most wants and it was tested hard before being rejected. amazon_returns.sku is a different key format from order_transaction.sku: only 699 of 1503 Amazon return SKUs (46%) exist in order_transaction AT ALL - verified all-time, so this is a key mismatch, not a timing gap. A rate column would be blank or wrong for over half the SKUs. It is computable for eBay alone (its SKU comes from order_transaction by construction) but a rate shown on one platform and not the other invites exactly the wrong comparison. Deliberately absent rather than misleading. Also unresolved: AMZ-PG-BAD-DESC is the 3rd-largest GBP refund reason (2340.74) and the largest USD reason - very likely the 'not as described' signal that routes to the Listing team, but merging it into NOT_AS_DESCRIBED is a taxonomy judgement for DWC.
evidence_refs: Amazon return SKUs 1503; match order_transaction 3mo 562, 12mo 610, all-time 699 (46%); any-channel all-time 711. AMZ-PG-BAD-DESC: GBP 2340.74 (3rd), USD 1289.18 (1st)
reusable_pattern: Refusing to ship a wanted metric is a valid deliverable. A column that is blank or wrong for half the rows is worse than its absence - it silently misleads.
validation_rule: Before offering a rate/ratio metric, prove the join key resolves for a large majority of rows. Below ~90% coverage, do not ship it.
gap_or_risk: HIGH - blocks return rate, revenue-at-risk and sell-through until someone reconciles the Amazon SKU key. MEDIUM - AMZ-PG-BAD-DESC taxonomy unresolved.
next_action: Someone must reconcile amazon_returns.sku with order_transaction.sku; DWC to rule on AMZ-PG-BAD-DESC

activity_title: Rebuilt the report as an interactive dashboard - 4 tabs, all records, no top-15 cap
activity_summary: Converted the long vertical report into a tabbed dashboard: Amazon/eBay x Reasons/SKUs, instant switching, no page reload. Removed the top-15 cap so ALL records load - Amazon SKUs went from 15 to 817 rows (GBP) and eBay from 15 to 310, 2106 records embedded in total. Added live search, filters (currency scope, reason, min/max refund, min returns), click-to-sort, pagination with 10/25/50/100/All, six KPI cards per platform that recompute as filters change, sticky headers, light/dark mode, CSV export of the visible tab, and print. Still a single self-contained file with zero external references. Currency remains a required single-select scope with no 'All' option, because an all-currencies view would put four currencies in one refund column.
evidence_refs: 2106 records embedded (Amazon reasons 66, Amazon SKUs 1580, eBay reasons 28, eBay SKUs 432); behavioural test suite passed - sort/filter/search/pagination/currency-isolation; validated top-15 preserved byte-identical as the first 15 rows
reusable_pattern: Top-3 highlighting must follow the RECORD, not the row position - a row keeps its flag when re-sorted, so the highlight never implies a ranking that is not real.
validation_rule: A currency filter must never offer an 'All' option where a money column is displayed.

activity_title: Added Units, Marketplace and Last Return after a fan-out safety test on each
activity_summary: Analysed the database for columns worth adding and tested each for fan-out risk before adding it. Units earns its place immediately - NOT_COMPATIBLE is 347 returns but 423 units, and the rank-1 SKU ENC6343 is a single return of 13 units, a bulk-return pattern the old table could not show. Marketplace was added to the SKU tabs only (verified 1:1 with the grain, zero fan-out) and deliberately NOT to the Reason tabs, where a reason is not a property of a marketplace and a dominant value would print 'UK' on every Amazon GBP row - a constant column carrying zero information. New fields were merged onto the existing validated rows BY KEY, not by position, so ranks and top-3 highlighting provably did not move: 2106 values re-checked, 0 moved.
evidence_refs: Fan-out test: fulfilment 0, sub_source_name 0 (safe); market_place 6, status 26, Category 69 (would split rows). Units reconcile exactly: Amazon 2789, eBay 794. Merge-by-key assertion: 2106 values, 0 moved, 0 unmatched.
reusable_pattern: Fan-out guard before adding any column: a new column is safe only if functionally determined by the existing grain. COUNT(DISTINCT new_col) per grain = 0 means safe; >0 means it can only appear as MODE() WITHIN GROUP (a dominant value).
validation_rule: When enriching an already-validated dataset, merge new fields onto the existing rows BY KEY, never by position - then assert 0 values moved.
gap_or_risk: label_cost (5157.26 of Amazon return postage, 83% populated) and eBay status (15 ESCALATED, 63 open) offered but not taken
next_action: Offer label_cost / true-cost and eBay escalation columns again

activity_title: Non-deterministic ORDER BY - 760 of 2106 rows reshuffle between identical runs
activity_summary: ORDER BY total_refunded DESC with no tie-break returns tied rows in an arbitrary order. Ties are common in refund data (many SKUs share an identical refund). Measured: 760 of 2106 rows change position between two identical runs of the same query. Cosmetic in a one-off report; a real defect once a daily cron is attached, because the dashboard would reshuffle itself AND renumber its ranks every morning with no data having changed. A deterministic tie-break (, sku / , reason) was added. Verified: all 760 moved rows are ties, ZERO moved for any other reason, every top-3 is identical, and refund-descending order still holds. Two consecutive refreshes now produce a byte-identical file, which they did not before.
evidence_refs: 760/2106 rows move without a tie-break; all 760 are ties, 0 moved for any other reason; top-3 unchanged in every table; md5 identical across two consecutive refreshes after the fix
reusable_pattern: Never ORDER BY a money column without a tie-break if the output is cached, embedded or scheduled. PostgreSQL is free to reorder ties, so the artefact churns without the data changing.
validation_rule: Every ORDER BY on a refund/money column must carry a deterministic tie-break (sku / reason).
next_action: Keep the tie-break in place through any future SQL edit

activity_title: Python daily refresh script - fails closed, atomic write, backups, logging
activity_summary: Built scripts/refresh_dashboard.py to re-run the four queries and rewrite ONLY the embedded const DATA block, preserving HTML/CSS/JS byte-for-byte. The dashboard is overwritten only after all four queries succeed, pass per-query row-count floors (a query returning zero or suspiciously few rows is refused as a broken filter, not written), and a structural fingerprint confirms nothing but the data block moved. Write is atomic via os.replace, every run keeps a timestamped backup, and every run appends start/finish/duration/rows/status/error to logs/dashboard_refresh.log. Failure paths tested for real: bad password and unreachable host both rollback, log the full error, leave the dashboard byte-for-byte intact and exit 1. Scheduled via cron at 09:00; the script contains no loop.
evidence_refs: 2106 rows in 1.65s, exit 0; failure paths (bad password, unreachable host) both exit 1 with dashboard md5 unchanged; two consecutive runs byte-identical (md5 13bb1f8fbf146b2376459aa7028cf6cb)
reusable_pattern: A scheduled job must FAIL CLOSED. Write only after every query succeeds, row-count floors pass, and a structural fingerprint proves nothing unintended moved. A cron job that overwrites a good artefact with a broken one is worse than one that does nothing.
validation_rule: Before any scheduled overwrite: assert row-count floors, assert structural fingerprint unchanged, back up, then write atomically.
next_action: Install the cron entry; monitor the first scheduled run

activity_title: Caught a stale SQL file that would have gutted the dashboard on its first cron run
activity_summary: Before wiring the scheduler, diffed sql/returns_hotspot_queries.sql against what the dashboard actually embeds. The SQL file was STALE - it still held the original top-15-capped queries with none of the later columns. Had cron executed it at 09:00, the Amazon SKU table would have dropped from 817 rows to 15 and the Units/Marketplace/Last Return columns would have gone blank: a silent, scheduled destruction of validated work, every morning, with a green exit code. The file was synced to the queries that actually built the validated dashboard - same filters, same res_his_order=0, same order_id+item_id join, same even-split, same currency separation, same CR- normalisation. No business logic changed.
evidence_refs: Stale file: 'WHERE rn <= 15' at lines 74 and 151, no units/market_place/MAX(request_date). Dashboard embeds 817 Amazon SKU rows and 8-field rows. Post-sync refresh reproduces the dashboard byte-identically.
reusable_pattern: Before attaching a scheduler to a script that reads a checked-in artefact (SQL, config, template), diff that artefact against what the live output actually contains. A stale input plus automation equals silent scheduled destruction with a green exit code.
validation_rule: Never schedule a job against a checked-in SQL/config file without first proving that file reproduces the current validated output.
gap_or_risk: Would have destroyed the dashboard on the first 09:00 run
next_action: Keep SQL file and dashboard in lockstep; the refresh script now proves it every run

activity_title: Credential migration to .env - and a credential exposure that must be rotated
activity_summary: Removed the hardcoded PostgreSQL credentials from refresh_dashboard.py. DB_CONFIG now loads from a gitignored, chmod 600 .env via python-dotenv with NO fallbacks - a missing variable prints its name, refuses to connect, and exits 1 (tested for all five). Added .env.example (placeholder password), .gitignore and requirements.txt. Verified the regenerated dashboard is byte-for-byte identical (same md5). Two deviations were necessary: load_dotenv() needs an EXPLICIT path because cron runs from $HOME and the bare call searches the cwd - it would have failed every morning; and pip install python-dotenv fails on this box under PEP 668, so the apt route is documented. ESCALATION: the database password was transmitted in plaintext in the task prompt and lived in the script's source before migration. temp_user must be treated as exposed and ROTATED.
evidence_refs: All 5 env vars validated individually (each exits 1 naming itself); md5 unchanged 13bb1f8fbf146b2376459aa7028cf6cb; grep for the password across every committable file returns zero matches; git check-ignore confirms .env excluded
reusable_pattern: load_dotenv() must be given an EXPLICIT path in any script that cron runs - the bare call searches the current working directory and cron runs from $HOME, so it silently finds nothing.
validation_rule: No fallback credentials in source, ever. A missing environment variable must fail loudly and exit non-zero, never quietly connect to a default.
gap_or_risk: CREDENTIAL EXPOSED - temp_user password was sent in plaintext and lived in source. Must be rotated. BLOS GAP - the 3-month window, top-3 rank and row-count floors are hardcoded business thresholds.
next_action: ROTATE the temp_user password; move the hardcoded thresholds into BLOS
